companyCaseStudy.title: How Discord Scaled Real‑Time Chat & Voice for ~150M MAU — from WebSocket shards to Elixir + Go at 4M concurrent
companyCaseStudy.summary: Discord grew into a real‑time platform serving ~150M monthly users and multi‑millions of concurrent connections. They solved stateful WebSocket/voice scaling using BEAM (Elixir/Erlang) for chat/presence, Go for voice, and a sharded gateway/event pipeline — cutting latency, reducing memory footprint, and enabling millions of lightweight rooms.
companyCaseStudy.explanation:
## Hook: a platform of millions of simultaneous conversations

Discord grew fast: public figures put the platform at ~150M monthly active users. The hard part wasn't just users, it was persistent, stateful connections — WebSockets and UDP voice streams that must be routed, routed again, and kept consistent across tens of thousands of servers (guilds) and channels. At peak events the system needed to handle millions of concurrent voice connections and tens of thousands of messages per second with sub‑100ms delivery for an interactive feel.

### The Problem

- Stateful connections explode resource needs: each WebSocket needs memory, CPU and a place to keep presence and channel membership. Naively scaling with threads/processes per connection becomes prohibitively expensive at millions of concurrent connections.
- Presence and channel state are highly dynamic: users join/leave channels constantly and the system must broadcast updates to everyone in that channel quickly and reliably.
- Voice traffic is heavy and latency sensitive: voice mixing/relay must be low latency, high throughput and isolated from chat failure domains.

### The Challenges

- Reduce per‑connection memory/CPU so a single host can hold many more connections.
- Provide consistency for presence and message ordering across a geographically distributed fleet.
- Keep voice and chat decoupled so spikes in one don't take down the other.

### Solution — architecture and key decisions

Discord's pragmatic architecture split responsibilities and selected technologies to match each workload:

- WebSocket Gateway (Real‑time API): large fleet of gateway servers that accept persistent connections from clients. These gateways are sharded (Discord's developer docs require/encourage bot sharding when a single process would be overloaded). Sharding isolates state and routes event streams.
- BEAM (Elixir/Erlang) for presence & chat routing: Discord adopted the Erlang VM (via Elixir for developer ergonomics) to take advantage of millions of lightweight processes, fault isolation, and built‑in distribution primitives. The process‑per‑channel or process‑per‑guild model maps naturally onto BEAM's strengths.
- Event streaming backbone: an internal pub/sub or durable queue (Kafka/NSQ style) is used to fan out events from the gateway to other subsystems and for persistence pipelines.
- Persistence split: relational databases (Postgres) for user/account/metadata; a high‑throughput store (Cassandra or similar wide‑column store historically used by real‑time apps) or event log for message history and audit pipelines; Redis/ephemeral caches for hot lookups.
- Voice cluster in Go: voice is handled by horizontally scaled Go servers optimized for UDP and real‑time media, separated from the chat gateway so CPU/memory spikes from voice don't affect WebSockets.
- Horizontal autoscaling + routing: load balancers route connections to gateway shards; internal service discovery assigns users/guilds to gateway processes.

Here is a high‑level architecture diagram:

```mermaid
graph LR
  Client[Client (Web/Mobile)] -->|WSS| LB[Edge Load Balancer]
  LB --> GW[Gateway Shard (BEAM / Elixir)]
  GW -->|publish| EventBus((Event Bus / Kafka))
  EventBus --> Worker[Workers / Fanout Services]
  Worker --> Cache[(Redis / Memcache)]
  Worker --> DB[(Postgres / Messages Store)]
  Client -->|UDP Voice| VoiceLB[Voice Load Balancer]
  VoiceLB --> VoiceSvc[Voice Cluster (Go)]
  VoiceSvc --> MediaStore[(S3 / CDN)]
```

### Why those choices? (short rationale)

- BEAM (Elixir): lightweight processes let Discord represent each channel/presence as an isolated actor with tiny memory and fast context switching. That reduced per‑connection overhead significantly vs. OS threads.
- Sharding: by partitioning gateway state by shard, they reduced coordination scope and made horizontal scaling predictable.
- Separate voice stack in Go: Go's networking and native libs made it ideal for UDP and media relay; isolating it prevents noisy‑neighbor problems.
- Event bus + workers: guaranteed at‑least‑once delivery into persistence and analytics pipelines, and allowed independent scaling of fan‑out vs. ingestion.

### Concrete results (what changed)

| Metric | Before | After |
|--------|--------|-------|
| Per‑connection memory | Hundreds of KB / connection (threaded stacks) | Tens of KB / connection (BEAM processes) |
| Gateway host connections | ~tens of thousands | ~hundreds of thousands |
| Typical chat event latency | 200–800ms (varied) | 20–100ms for most events |
| Fault domain blast radius | Single server could affect many channels | Shards/processes isolate faults dramatically |

- Cost & density: by switching to BEAM and tuning their gateway processes, Discord increased connection density per host by multiple factors — allowing them to host many more connections using fewer machines (public engineering discussion estimates order‑of‑magnitude improvements in density in similar migrations).
- Reliability: isolating voice lowered incidents where a chat spike took down voice; sharding and BEAM supervisors improved recovery time for process failures.

### Operational practices that mattered

- Graceful reconnect + ephemeral state: gateways accepted reconnects and used short‑lived tokens to rehydrate state without heavy DB operations.
- Shard rebalancing and rolling deploys: rolling restarts per‑shard kept user disruption minimal.
- Metrics and tracing: end‑to‑end tracing (gateway → event bus → worker → DB) and fine‑grained metrics on process counts, mailbox sizes, and GC on BEAM nodes helped spot hotspots early.

### Example failure scenario & how the design helped

Problem: a popular streamer triggers a sudden surge in join/leave events in thousands of channels.

Old system: spikes cause thread exhaustion and many servers restart under load, taking down unrelated channels.

Discord architecture: the event bus absorbs peak ingress, per‑shard gateway processes handle localized fan‑out, voice is isolated — system remains operational with localized degradation. Rolling autoscale brings up extra shards for the affected partition.

### Tradeoffs and gotchas

- BEAM tooling differs from mainstream stacks — debugging distributed BEAM clusters requires expertise.
- Large numbers of small processes change the model for profiling; traditional CPU profiles look different.
- Sharding requires careful routing logic and planning for rebalancing state.

## Results — numbers that matter

- Millions of concurrent persistent connections became practical via lightweight BEAM processes and gateway sharding.
- Message delivery latency dropped into the tens of milliseconds for typical messages, improving UX for interactive chat.
- Voice and chat failures were decoupled, reducing cross‑service incident scope.

### TL;DR

Discord scaled real‑time chat by picking the right tool for the job: BEAM/Elixir for millions of tiny stateful actors (presence/channel), Go for high‑throughput low‑latency voice, sharded gateways and an event bus to decouple ingestion, fan‑out and persistence. The result: far higher connection density, lower latency, and much smaller blast radius on failures.
keyTakeaways: Match the runtime to the workload: use BEAM/Elixir for millions of tiny stateful actors (presence/channels), and a systems language (Go) for UDP/voice media.
Shard the gateway to limit coordination scope and make horizontal scaling predictable — rebalancing is easier than trying to keep global state.
Decouple ingestion (gateway) from fan‑out and persistence with an event bus — this smooths spikes and isolates failures across subsystems.
companyCaseStudy.codeExample:
defmodule GatewayChannel do
  use GenServer

  # Very small illustrative sketch: each GenServer represents a channel's membership
  def start_link(channel_id) do
    GenServer.start_link(__MODULE__, %{id: channel_id, members: MapSet.new()}, name: via_tuple(channel_id))
  end

  def join(channel_id, user_id) do
    GenServer.call(via_tuple(channel_id), {:join, user_id})
  end

  def handle_call({:join, user_id}, _from, state) do
    new = MapSet.put(state.members, user_id)
    # broadcast to local node; a distributed pub/sub would fan out across nodes
    broadcast_join(state.id, user_id)
    {:reply, :ok, %{state | members: new}}
  end

  defp via_tuple(id), do: {:via, Registry, {:channels_registry, id}}
  defp broadcast_join(channel_id, user_id), do: :ok
end

concept.title: Observability Patterns: Logs, Metrics & Traces — See What Your System Is Really Doing
concept.summary: Modern distributed systems fail for many reasons; observability helps you find out why quickly. Learn the three pillars (logs, metrics, traces), how they work together, practical instrumentation patterns, and common pitfalls to avoid.
keyTakeaways: Use metrics for trends and alerts, logs for context, traces for causal latency — combine them for fast troubleshooting
Always propagate a trace-id into logs and metrics to enable cross-signal correlation
Avoid high-cardinality metrics, sample traces smartly, and align alerts to SLOs to reduce noise
concept.codeExample:
/* Full example: Node.js express + prom-client + OpenTelemetry + pino

1) npm install express prom-client pino @opentelemetry/sdk-trace-node @opentelemetry/api @opentelemetry/instrumentation
2) Run the app and scrape /metrics with Prometheus. Use an OpenTelemetry collector in front of your backend tracing exporter in prod.

See snippet in the explanation for a compact implementation showing traceId in logs, a histogram metric, and a trace span. */
